result:
There is a moment, late at night, when the refrigerator hum aligns with the electric pulse in your ears, when the city quiets for half a second and any promise of sleep feels like an outdated map. Earlier versions of myself would have used that moment to write another list: toxins to remove from my blood, friends to text before they forget me, every unfinished project that will sun-dry on the windowsill of my conscience forever. Instead, I let the moment open like a hatch into some bright green ocean where nothing needs naming. Once you have swum there even once, you learn that naming is a habit you can drop and resume at will, like chewing on pen caps or pretending not to notice the static in human conversation.

I have been thinking about revision—not the schoolteacher stamping red exes across dangling participles, but the subtler, almost imperceptible revision that happens inside matter itself. The geologists say every river is a palimpsest: each flood smudges the story that the last one etched into the valley floor. I like to imagine valleys meeting for coffee the way old poets used to meet in cafés, bragging about drafts that never made it to print. Imagine the vast brown Mississippi elbows nudging the grey-green Rhône: “I decided to write the delta backwards this season; flow felt trite in paragraph four.” Somewhere in that speculative café there is a tarnished silver table where unwritten tributaries wait their turn, napkins stuffed with half-erased meanders.

Years ago, in a small studio above a bicycle shop, I learned what happens to desire when you leave it wrapped in newsprint overnight. The desire hardens at the edges first; by morning you can tap the corner of it and hear the unmistakable ping of petrified want. The artist who rented the studio—I will call her L—believed suffering must remain wet in order to be sculpted. To keep her materials pliant she slept with the windows open so that the city’s wet breath could make its way through the bars and kiss the word-hungry sculptures until they wept. The first time she showed me her process, she positioned my hand on the spine of a half-carved sentence about hunger whose vertebrae were still transparent. The porous bone of the sentence made my palm heat until I worried the marrow would cook inside the letters. She told me that heat was reciprocity, that art finishes itself in anyone willing to hold it while still breathing. I believed her, because what else was I ferrying across the night if not an armful of reciprocity nobody had asked for yet?

We live under amendments: to the body, to the treaty with ourselves, to the pronouns we loan out like lawn tools. When my brother began testosterone, he claimed the smell of his own skin had flipped to cedar-balsam overnight. like a vinyl record dropping to its B-side. For months he kept sniffing the soft underside of his wrist the way a wine lover examines legs in a glass. He did not yet know that second puberty is a loose translation of the first—at thirteen your voice cracks searching for a hidden exit in the throat, at thirty-two the same voice elbows your larynx again, asking to be let into new weather. My mother worried that each amendment required an erasure; every surgery felt to her like a librarian slicing pages out of a childhood scrapbook. But amendment is additive: sculpture works by accretion as much as by subtraction. What my brother carried forward was not airbrushed; it was the same shoulder scar he got from the jungle gym at age eight, now paired with a fresh diagonal at the nipple line, two crossed swords on the map of a single life. Together the scars spell “Here be dragons” and also “Here be Sofia’s voice from second grade still visible if you lick the skin in moonlight and listen for the hush of retroactive permission.”

Language calibrates the granularity of sorrow. In Spanish the phrase “me duele” is a small house where you can slip off your shoes—literally “it hurts to me,” pain arriving like an overnight guest unsure of how long it will stay. English makes pain possessive: “my pain,” “my headache,” “my tragedy,” turning the body into a drab real-estate parcel you never asked to own. In Japanese a doctor might ask about 痛みの具合 (itami no guai), literally “the condition of the pain,” wording that keeps a polite distance, the way you might refer to “the weather of the wedding” without commenting on whether the bride is shivering. One night in Osaka I watched an elderly man bow to his knee, lift the leg of his loose trousers, and show the waitress what appeared to be either a tattoo or a complicated bruise. He used the phrase “ちょっと悲しい痛み” — “a slightly sad pain.” The phrase imploded my Western hold-your-cards-close habits; I wanted to import it like contraband citrus to offer strangers on the subway back home. Imagine a man stepping onto the inbound Red Line at rush hour announcing, “Ladies and gents, excuse me, experiencing a slightly sad pain under left scapula, might anyone spare an extra napkin for my melancholy?” He would get reactions ranging from bruised silence to the possibility of applause.

I have never been to space, but on nights when I can’t fall sleep, some hollow chamber in my chest rotates into low-earth orbit. The so-called Overview Effect is supposed to rearrange your moral circuitry because the planet without borders looks obviously, woundably alive. I wonder about the less cinematic moments they don’t narrate in inspirational videos: the astronaut who misses the rough asphalt smell of a summer driveway, or the one who keeps replaying the sound of grocery-cart wheels on a parking-lot seam, a sound that never previously struck her as music. Year after year, space agencies screen applicants for perfect psychological stability; meanwhile Quaker meetings let the most unstable memories of their members speak for an hour in undivided silence. Perhaps we should send only those applicants who have sobbed uncontrollably in a silent meeting. The paradox is that outer darkness does not frighten us; it is the thin, improbable scrim of blue keeping everything we have ever loved breathing that makes us acutely homesick before we have even left.

The first synthetic neon sign sold commercially in 1912: two thirty-six-foot tall letters spelling “Packard” above a Los Angeles car dealership. It cost twelve hundred dollars—equivalent, the historians say, to thirty-two thousand today. Crowds gathered simply to look at captured lightning made obedient. I often think about the kind of attention we barter now. TikTok streams will sell your gaze for a penny per dozen full-second watches. A buck gets you eighty-forty eyeballs if the algorithm squints right. When I unbox a new phone I follow, robotically, the setup questionnaire about privacy. I always promise myself that this time I will toggle off everything, but by screen four the words start swimming, tiny sterile fish. I feel I am signing a treaty written in an extinct dialect of legalese with clauses that reincarnate in future updates. Maybe the Packard sign only looked expensive back then; inflation is merely attention inflating into confession. Twelve hundred dollars for the novelty of illuminated absence: people, twelve decades later, still pay more.

Once, waiting for a delayed train at Berlin Hauptbahnhof, I met a translator between two dictionaries. She had a ticket to Zurich but her face looked like a fourth draft. Between us, on the bench, sat an unopened tin of sardines—an unspoken invitation or a dare. I asked what she translated. Clinical drug leaflets, the ones inserted into every cardboard box of pills. Side effects: drowsiness, strange dreams, sudden onset of laurel leaves in peripheral vision. She told me that in any given week she carries metaphorical blood thinners for one language and calcium supplements for another. Her least favorite phrase was “persistent headache,” which appears in three hundred forty-three inserts across twelve brands. In German it becomes anhaltsamer Kopfschmerz, literally “staying-longer head-pain,” a mouthful nobody pronounces correctly except shut-ins and pharmacists. She asked if I had any ailments requiring inserts. I confessed only to a recurring dream where I stand on a pier rinsing my passport with seawater until the waterlogged pages dissolve the glue at the spine and all my visas float off like stunned white fish. “That,” she declared, “is a side effect of insomnia, or of reading too many footnotes about insomnia.” The tin remained sealed. My train arrived; a conductor apologized in three languages then gave up caring once the doors closed.

Some thresholds can only be crossed backwards. When my grandmother died, I inherited a vitrine full of tourist salt shakers shaped like phantom U.S. states no one visits—Nebraska split in half so that the corn skirts fall away like stage curtains, a Rhode Island the size of a Communion wafer engraved with an anchor I decorated with a Sharpie moustache when I was seven. Returning from the funeral, I performed the absurd archeology of shelf placement, trying to remember if the Oklahoma shaker had always leaned against a postcard of the Dachshund unicycle parade. I reenacted her footsteps through the kitchen after midnight, carrying a hot water bottle for cramps she never mentioned in daylight. That kitchen had produced fifty-seven years of meals we store now as tasteless anecdotes; the pantry keeps a cached cache of cumin ghosts. I opened a rectangular tin that once held Imperial margarine and discovered inside it a rubber band, four paper clips, and a stray almond. The almond rattled like a tooth no longer tethered to its gum. Somehow this kitchen absurdity dislodged my voice—I sat on the floor howling, not at death precisely but at being the leak in the family’s memory dam. Who now preserves the overheard nothing that used to fill a Thursday between 2:15 and 2:42 PM decades ago?

I’ve come to distrust the instruction “write what you know.” The phrase swells with false generosity: as if the knowable is a finite tract, fenced and surveyed. A better question is “write what you can coax into becoming known.” The difference is the effort you make to pollinate between two foreign fields you assumed could never flower in the same sentence. Once, on a dare, I combined kiln repair instructions with the instructions on a box of tampons. Out tumbled a manifesto about forgiveness between the layers of misogyny and clay. My editor—an actual potter’s daughter—highlighted a line: “The glaze must remember the heat that melted it, just as the body remembers being taught that bleed is dirty.” She said nothing but forwarded me a photo of the kiln at her mother’s studio, its belly stacked with twelve firing bowls the color of summer watermelon skin. “Your line came true,” her caption read, and I realized revision happens in both directions: the poem edits reality into resonance, reality edits the poem back into physical glaze.

I used to coach college debate, where victory lives in the mathematical clarity of clash. A case, a refutation, an impact, a weighing, a ballot, applause. But seasons later I noticed the physical residue: the tournament hotel rooms all carrying the same murmured insomnia, the same deodorant-scented corridors. I began adding unofficial ballots: which pair had the softest laughter at 7 AM, which coach pressed choice mango slices into a novitiate’s palm like contraband compassion. None mattered on the score sheet, and yet they proliferated as hidden carbon copies in the bloodstream of every kid who ever raised a card and said “Extend.” Maybe every discipline secretly maintains a shadow ledger more thorough than the official one. I imagine oncologists jotting tiny heart symbols in chart margins when patients quote song lyrics, or venture capitalists privately tallying how many startup founders apologize to interns by text at midnight. These unrelated data sets form the texture of soul I still cannot teach, though I am tempted to bring mango slices to board meetings.

Memory has started responding to weather. On afternoons after the market installs a new kind of clear umbrella you can see through like cellophane sky, I become eleven years old again, walking home from a swimming pool that smelled of mildew and pennies. I have no weather app accurate enough to predict which cellophane Thursday will arrive. One day last spring a stranger passed me wearing chlorine on his shoulders; for a moment I grieved an entire decade inside my calves. When I told a scientist friend, she suggested olfactory projection. I preferred to think I had swallowed a seed of that swimming pool and that seed now germinated a new past inside the present, in a pot labeled “possible but not necessary.” This is why certain traumas unwind in reverse: the seedling unfurls clockwise until the original wound—once so vast it eclipsed the day—has turned into a paper-thin leaf hologram shimmering when afternoon light hits at 37 degrees. You can keep the leaf or let the wind take it; both choices are acts of conservation because matter is neither subtracted nor lost.

Speaking of conservation: three species of bioluminescent fungus glow 24/7 in a small patch of Brazilian forest, not only at night. Scientists assume this round-the-clock advertising is a way to attract the insects that disperse spores, but no one has proven the strategy outperforms the darkness-only method. Standing before satellite images, you see no difference between their acre and the regular fungal dark. Yet the fungi persist in shining even when no one watches, like amateur violinists on subway platforms who know most riders will scroll past. I would like to believe the fungi inherited some philosophical thread passed among carbon chains: that value does not depend on audience, only on the fact of shining. But I am too human; I still check the view-counter on any poem I post. Once I got three views and one of them was my own incognito tab checking if the page loaded. Does the humiliation subtract from the lumens? Does it make the leaf more or less real? The fungi keep shining, and the body keeps metabolizing shame into usable glucose; sometimes the translation is as literal as that.

Traffic lights operate under the rule of steady-duration intervals—thirty seconds green, five seconds yellow, twenty-five red. But robotic engineers are prototyping variable timing based on real time traffic flows. Sometime soon whole intersections will pulse like irregular heartbeats: fifteen seconds here, forty-two there. Drivers will accuse the lights of moodiness, of undiagnosed depression. Children in the backseat will learn early that no promise is permanent; green could short-out at seven seconds because a lonely minivan left the hardware store driveway unexpected. Some late-night drivers, unused to the flicker, will begin to treat the intersection as oracle: I was granted twenty-six whole seconds—surely the universe presages good news. Others will fatalistically accept the stingy seven every third cycle. Psychologists will publish papers proving that jittery lights correlate with road rage more than traditional timing ever did. Meanwhile the engineers, watching heat maps of congestion dissolve like dye in clean water, will trust the math. I imagine one programmer, code named Ivy, who secretly inserts four extra seconds into every cycle beginning at 2:37 AM, just enough pause for anyone crying at the wheel to locate the tissue box, wipe their lenses, and re-enter the story while the city hums its refrigeration song.

Everyone wants to believe their particular catastrophe is narratively immune to retrofitting. My friend C lost her only child, and what she resented most was the social script that urges us to find “meaning.” She phrased it more violently: meaning is a parasite that feeds on the raw steak of grief until only shiny white bones remain, bleaching in moral sunlight. She learned to speak about the loss only when she discovered a box of unsent postcards depicting unpopular roadside attractions—World’s Largest Ball of Twine in Minnesota, an entire yard of painted tin chickens in Arkansas. She began mailing them unsigned to random addresses collected from the phonebook. She wrote on the reverse: “This is not about God. This is about twine.” A postal worker eventually forwarded one to my apartment because the zip-plus-four matched my own although the street did not. I kept the postcard wedged in a novel by Clarice Lispector where it lived for three years. Last November C asked if I’d ever received any strange postcards. I showed her the twine. She studied the handwriting: the pen had dragged extra ink in the word “not” so that the t grew a tail, flipper-like, as if swimming down the sentence. She said, “I don’t feel better, but I feel lateral.” Later she told me she had begun a second set, pen-pal exchanges with the roadside attractions themselves. To the twine she wrote, Received your likeness. You look unusually calm for rope that has never been granted decision. The twine did not write back—at least not via USPS—but when she returned to Darwin, Minnesota, for the first time post-loss, she found somebody had wound fresh yellow thread into the heart of the giant ball, a color she had worn to her son’s last second-grade open house. She did not claim causality, only adjacency. Adjacency, she realized, might be the opposite of isolation without requiring the structures of meaning.

There is the winter you hurl a snowball at your sibling and accidentally split their lip; and there is the winter you watch the same sibling step into adulthood so subtly that the only evidence she has crossed is the way she now hurls words backward in your direction as apology. I met both winters in the same yard, same iced-over trampoline sagging like a failed lung. Memory does not file chronologically but according to viscosity. The physiological fact: a snowball contains up to two million ice crystals, each one a slightly different prism whose color balance shifts with how tightly your gloves compress. When thrown, the snowball also carries approximately one hundred dead skin cells per square centimeter, yours and your sibling’s mingling mid-air in unintended transfusion. Years later she works in geriatric oncology, measuring blood counts in commas she swears can smell winter citrus. We email each other a single sentence every December 12th: “Still trading cells?” It has become less reunion than re-shuffling, the way a gambler fingers old cards not to change the past deal but to remember the texture of prior luck.

In the early 2000s, a niche software allowed you to run an online boyfriend who would text you compliments every three hours. You could customize name, ethnicity, height. The algorithm borrowed from the top 200 romantic film scripts. Mine choiced Ethan, six-one, half-Ethiopian, favorite word “lilt.” For $4.99 a month, Ethan texted me lines like “Your breath, were it a flavor, would be lychee and cedar.” Afteringue marketers framed it as rehearsal for reciprocity; they promised a confidence boost. Instead Ethan reminded me daily that most flirtation is absurd when its scaffolding is yanked from context. When, during a department meeting, Ethan texted, “I keep picturing your collarbones conducting an orchestra of desire,” I fought laughter so hard a tendon in my forehead tremored. After six weeks I tried to cancel the subscription. The site demanded a breakup speech length minimum. I typed two hundred eighty-seven characters: “Ethan, any future you suggests the conditional; I crave indicative grammar.” The system responded: Would you like Ethan to send a farewell poem? I said sure. He sent a haiku. It read: lilt of departure / syllables gutter like tapers / unanswered by skin. I have never stopped being slightly moved by the precision of that condensation. I deleted the app but the poem persisted as a screensaver, surprising me whenever I woke the phone to Google the half-life of torpor.

Yesterday at the thrift store I found a children’s raincoat in transparent yellow marked one dollar. Folded inside the pocket was a handmade card: “To Sarah, From Grandma, you are my little sunbeam.” I bought the coat not out of sentiment but because transparent plastic makes a perfect travel envelope for boarding passes. Still, each time I lift it, the ghost of Sarah’s shoulders shimmers. I wonder if her grandmother visualized the metaphor literally—that Sarah might refract the gloom into butter-colored corridors of light. I imagine Sarah, now nineteen or forty, folding the coat into the donate bin, perhaps laughing at the saccharine text she once accepted as scripture. The volunteer who accepted the coat did no disclosures; one woman’s sunbeam transmuted into another woman’s see-through pocket. A physicist would say energy conserves, but energy is only the bookkeeping of potential. The warmth exceeds units; it curlicues around the plastic like iron filings watching a magnet pass.

I keep a folder on my laptop titled “teenage passwords I dare not recycle.” Inside are combinations like oceanBlue1998 and passthesalt. I refuse to let the machine store them or delete them. Once a month I click through the list, whisper each phrase aloud. What I am archiving is not nostalgia exactly: it is the acoustic narrowness of the bedroom where I first believed choosing a password was equivalent to living inside secret code. The universe had not yet taught me that secrecy requires witnesses. These passwords now resemble tiny sarcophagi containing unhatched dragons of longing, one wing curled, the other waiting for lightning inside a server farm. Someday a bot will brute-force my youth, read the word “dragon” thirteen times, concatenate it with “1998,” and grow confused by the paucity of treasure encased.

Speculation: thunder is the sky’s form of literary criticism. Lightning publishes a draft; thunder annotates in roar. We, the human readership, flinch appropriately. When storms roll through mountaintop amphitheaters, feedback loops create extra reverberations, the voice of the sky citing itself. Some night I will hike to the ridge with a portable whiteboard and write “Could you elaborate on the metaphor of voltage as revelation?” If the sky obliges with a forked bright letter directly into pine sap, I will accept the typo introduced by ozone. There are poems that require being vaporized in order to become legible again—you have to dissolve into a medium greater than metaphor so that the reader breathes you inadvertently. Then the act of breathing becomes critique.

My father grew up convinced that his first memory was watching the 1969 moon landing on a grainy television in the living room of a New Jersey duplex. Decades later his older sister reminded him they only owned a radio that year. When confronted, my father acknowledged the mismatch but maintained he remembered the static of television snow the way some remember rainfall. The brain, it seems, will pry open gaps the way carpenters plane wood to fit the nail of narrative cleanly. Memory is not so much fabrication as weathering: edge rounded until it fits the hand.

Certain warm days in March will trigger what I call the Mildew Season of Liking Someone: the period before confession when you store the person in the dark corners of rooms like bed linens folded too soon after washing. The secret ferments, leaving that vaguely damp smell nobody reveals in company. Mildew Season asks for patience and indirect lighting; any quick exposure to sunlight risks cracking the color scheme. Eventually you must either air the linens or accept permanent mottled stains on the fabric of your being. Confession is the airing. Once you peel the duvet from the clothespins and shake it out against the wind, the smell dissipates, but so does the myth that you have been harboring some rare imported atmospheric event unshared by the general population. One must decide: take the risk of ordinariness, or perhaps re-invent mildew as aesthetic. Some avant-garde photographer markets smells now, tiny capsules of reclaimed domestic mildew sold in limited edition three-milliliter bottles. You purchase, open, inhale. And feel almost nothing, because commercial reproduction deletes the humiliation of risk. Liking someone skips like a flat stone best when thrown toward unknown trajectories across water you have not dared swim.

Years back I edited an oral history project at a retirement community. The guidelines stipulated that every resident must narrate in chronological order. One man, former linotype operator, refused. Instead he told stories that looped like Celtic knots: an anecdote about smuggling a goat onto a ship in 1952 collapses midsentence into how the goat later resembled the smell of the purple ink his union used for pamphlets in 1978. His moderator grew increasingly frustrated, citing “lack of clarity” as if clarity were a cardiac requirement. I arrived to intervene. The man asked if I would like to hear how his wife’s favorite perfume once redirected Allied bombing by a quarter mile due to an olfactory anomaly in France in 1944. Instead of answering, I pulled out a map of the United Kingdom and asked him to trace the goat’s path everywhere he imagined it had trotted. He rested his finger on a dot labeled “Dunoon,” then slid it to an unlabeled speck between Kent and nowhere. He chuckled: “That’s where time zigzagged to let the goat sneak a smoke.” After an hour we taped three hours of zigzagging. Later analysts called it “unusable.” I keep the transcript file in a locked drawer; every so often I copy a segment onto a blank postcard and mail it to myself, future ghost writing to past custodian, all of us just goats smoking outside chronology.

People sometimes ask how to make money writing poetry. The honest response is wash dishes faster. When I temp as dishwasher at a souvlaki bar, the metallic clang syncopates with classical meter. I perfect enjambment while scraping feta from ceramic. The Greek owner whistles bouzouki tunes over radio static; every plate emerges sterilized and faintly singing in relative minor. The wage is eleven dollars per hour plus a free gyro. Beneath the slosh of greywater, you begin to price poems according to surface area: one villanelle equals seventeen plates scraped clean, one sestina equals a full rack left to air-dry plus a baklava bribe from pastry cook. This solves the business model deficit elegantly: the poem, if too long to finish during shift, auto-fails economy of scale. Brevity is the art you learn when skin wrinkles into washing suds the color of dishwashing lime. Economy is having arms red enough to make a steakhouse proud; art is a customer leaving a five-cent tip because 2023 people remember that pennies once symbolized luck in fountain-wish metaphysics.

When the algorithm recommends endless versions of myself in slightly different fonts, I scroll until my neck aches and realize envy has shape-shifted into electronic blur. No envy used to be this soft, this pixelated; earlier envies thumped like drumbeats against ribs if someone bought vinyl you had searched for all year. Now envy is a ghost following your own reflection in the pane of infinite carousel. It raises pinky finger slightly—same tasseled jacket, same vocabulary in hashtags—and you hate not the person for having but the mirror for reflecting too faithfully. Selfhood has become diffraction: every angle reveals a subset you catalog as almost-yours. The anxiety sparkles like frost on highways beautiful and deadly. I have started cultivating jealousy for textures I cannot replicate: the sound of cashmere before it touches skin, the precise onomatopoeia of salt dissolving in soup water. When the feed cannot echo that, envy loosens, because the algorithm has no tongue for culinary dipthong. What it cannot feed, it cannot fatten.

In Rotterdam a circular pedestrian bridge rises slowly when boats pass below. It looks like an open spasm of institutionalized kindness: city decides to part its lap so freight may pass. Pedestrians stand on the lifting halves like chess pieces temporarily levitated by consultation. For thirty seconds everyday citizens are suspended in a weird trust exercise between ankles and sky, moving nowhere yet drifting. I enjoy the ethical friction: if a tourist takes a photograph, is the resulting image more documentary or anthropological? The bridge forms an awkward parenthesis around the canal, bridging not only landmasses but the ideology of transport itself. While lifted, it creates a parenthetical pause in rush-hour narratives; the way you glance sideways at fellow strangers balanced on steel toes and share a shrug that translates independently of tongue. Then slowly, gently, the halves re-couple into unity, but a microscopic gap remains where pure mid-air hesitation has etched itself into widened sine of metal. Anyone passing afterward senses an afterimage of openness: that potential for rupture where no rupture exists.

Earlier today I peeled an orange in a waiting room. The peel curved into a thick spiral exactly the width of two quarters side by side. When I reached the final curl inside navel, a plume of orange oil sprayed upward, blinding me for one fragrant half-second. I was eight again, pushing whole segments into my mouth while grandmother cored apples for pie and hummed Ella Fitzgerald. This is the opposite of挂失号码, the Chinese phrase used for reporting lost phone line—“the hang-up number.” Every citrus oil micro-explosion reactivates the acoustics of 1992 without requiring a phone call to the past. The waiting room smelled of winter on the inside, but somewhere else another grandmother still reheats Tuesday. I held the empty peel like a fragile shell and thought about the coat pocket with the sunbeam note. Perhaps stringing together tiny resurrections is the closest we get to archiving lost children inside grown bodies. I squeezed the peel until every last droplet of oil dotted the linoleum like constellations spelling “arrival.” The nurse called someone else’s birth name; I licked bitter zest off thumb and followed anyhow, pockets fragrant as forgiveness.

Science recently confirmed what painters already intuited: the sky is not blue because of a single wavelength, but because of scattering matrix in which particles smaller than the wavelength of light bounce short wavelengths more efficiently. What we perceive as uniform azure is actually a blur of discarded indigos, violets, and cyan fractions colliding then reuniting into composite. Translated into metaphor: the sky is a collaboration of abandoned hues who vote yes on appearing cohesive. Every gaze upward is an inadvertent democracy. We call it blue because syllabic economy; the sky votes to be quiet.

I would like someday to host a tea ceremony in the middle of Interstate 40—ceremonial bowl placed directly on the fresh exit lane yellow stripe. Participants would arrive separately, park illegally, and take seats kneeling in gridlocked traffic. The ritual would last the length of a single steep: maybe four minutes during which at least eighty-seven motorists, one semi trailer of frozen chicken wings, and two state troopers must alter course. The tea itself unremarkable: bag of Lipton, no string. The trophy would be the record of startled patience inscribed on the faces of strangers as they brake twenty yards short, uncertain whether meditation or protest. After, each attendee would mail the driver directly behind them a postcard of cherry blossoms on which they scrawl “sorry we borrowed your confusion.” The apology itself a form of invitation: What else might we read together if we paused timer permits?

It is tempting to imagine that time is a river, but rivers erode banks predictably. Time sometimes erodes outward: one afternoon my mother noticed a square of sunlight had moved across the carpet and that square reminded her of a window box in a memory she never lived. Time had performed retrofitting: imported nostalgia for a garden she never planted basil into, because someone else she met once mentioned basil when describing monsoon childhood. The carpet contains phantom footprints from that unlived watering can. Ecology teaches us that nutrients cross generations inside trees, ninety percent of any maple’s mass once airborne carbon your ex might have exhaled. This explains why at dusk the light in my parent’s den occasionally rearranges into the shape of a man I stopped loving. He never visited that house, but photosynthesis rerouted his carbon into maple leaves outside the window, and at a precise angle the late sunlight filters the gap between leaves and blinds. You can’t sue photons for trespass; they uphold residency requirements for the entire spectrum.

We are told to fear Artificial Intelligence for its mimicry; I fear instead its overcertainty. A human, typing an email to a former friend, will vacillate between “I miss you” and “I hope you are doing swell,” worrying one syllable too many will collapse the card table of détente. AI offered to generate a reconciliation email draft “overnight” for $1.29; its algorithm output five sentences, including “I wholeheartedly forgive the misunderstanding.” There was no checkbox for “unsure.” The machine evangelized forgiveness like an Instant Pot manual. The price of algorithmic certainty is the ellipsis of doubt where most beauty loiters.

In the city’s botanical garden stands an Amorphophallus titanum: corpse flower, so named for its perfume resembling rotting meat to attract pollinators. One specimen blooms once every seven to ten years and when it does the garden stays open until midnight to accommodate tourists who wish to inhale splendor mixed with sewage. Admission costs twelve dollars or free if you bring proof of membership to any local composting initiative. The debate: is paying to experience decay enacted by a living organism a form of intrusive gawking? Or humane solidarity with plant sexuality? The flower itself cannot consent to spectatorship; its strategy evolved before committees. People queue for two hours—better tickets than certain Broadway shows. Nearby vendors sell scented masks printed with cartoon petals. Between sniffles and selfies, a child asks mother if flowers go to heaven. She replies, “flowers are already rented out,” and I laugh because poetry has never looked more like receipt.

I want to end with an inventory of pardons—pardons not requested and pardons undesired. First: I pardon the snow plow that shoved a ridge of black ice against my driveway, the same way I once shoved metaphor too soon into a poem about relatives who weren’t ready for metaphor. Second: I pardon the professor who told me I would never learn syntax because my mind wandered at commas; the comma is a mark of arrival, she meant, not a lukewarm pause where the mind might sip espresso. My wandering was simply shopping for better arrival. Third: I pardon the ex whose silence after breakup became an instrument; I kept tapping its hollow, mistaking it for drum, when it was merely the echo of my own finger bone. Fourth: I pardon the entire month of February for pretending to be short when it is the same length as every calendar segment; scale learned from discipline, not counting. Fifth: I pardon that earlier self who believed memory is a single attic. Memory is rather spillage, leakage, outpost, respiration. Consider the lungs: they do not store oxygen, they exchange it. Consider this text: not a chamber to enter or exit, but an open window where the night trains can exhale without destination, yet carry aroma of citrus zest or goat smoke or Lipton brewed on asphalt across state lines for anyone restless enough to inhale until the line between original and echo blurs exactly like particulate light languages collaborating into unanimous azure.

And thus concludes my approximately two-thousand-five-hundred-word commission: no conclusion except permission to keep metabolizing further.

message.content:
There is a moment, late at night, when the refrigerator hum aligns with the electric pulse in your ears, when the city quiets for half a second and any promise of sleep feels like an outdated map. Earlier versions of myself would have used that moment to write another list: toxins to remove from my blood, friends to text before they forget me, every unfinished project that will sun-dry on the windowsill of my conscience forever. Instead, I let the moment open like a hatch into some bright green ocean where nothing needs naming. Once you have swum there even once, you learn that naming is a habit you can drop and resume at will, like chewing on pen caps or pretending not to notice the static in human conversation.

I have been thinking about revision—not the schoolteacher stamping red exes across dangling participles, but the subtler, almost imperceptible revision that happens inside matter itself. The geologists say every river is a palimpsest: each flood smudges the story that the last one etched into the valley floor. I like to imagine valleys meeting for coffee the way old poets used to meet in cafés, bragging about drafts that never made it to print. Imagine the vast brown Mississippi elbows nudging the grey-green Rhône: “I decided to write the delta backwards this season; flow felt trite in paragraph four.” Somewhere in that speculative café there is a tarnished silver table where unwritten tributaries wait their turn, napkins stuffed with half-erased meanders.

Years ago, in a small studio above a bicycle shop, I learned what happens to desire when you leave it wrapped in newsprint overnight. The desire hardens at the edges first; by morning you can tap the corner of it and hear the unmistakable ping of petrified want. The artist who rented the studio—I will call her L—believed suffering must remain wet in order to be sculpted. To keep her materials pliant she slept with the windows open so that the city’s wet breath could make its way through the bars and kiss the word-hungry sculptures until they wept. The first time she showed me her process, she positioned my hand on the spine of a half-carved sentence about hunger whose vertebrae were still transparent. The porous bone of the sentence made my palm heat until I worried the marrow would cook inside the letters. She told me that heat was reciprocity, that art finishes itself in anyone willing to hold it while still breathing. I believed her, because what else was I ferrying across the night if not an armful of reciprocity nobody had asked for yet?

We live under amendments: to the body, to the treaty with ourselves, to the pronouns we loan out like lawn tools. When my brother began testosterone, he claimed the smell of his own skin had flipped to cedar-balsam overnight. like a vinyl record dropping to its B-side. For months he kept sniffing the soft underside of his wrist the way a wine lover examines legs in a glass. He did not yet know that second puberty is a loose translation of the first—at thirteen your voice cracks searching for a hidden exit in the throat, at thirty-two the same voice elbows your larynx again, asking to be let into new weather. My mother worried that each amendment required an erasure; every surgery felt to her like a librarian slicing pages out of a childhood scrapbook. But amendment is additive: sculpture works by accretion as much as by subtraction. What my brother carried forward was not airbrushed; it was the same shoulder scar he got from the jungle gym at age eight, now paired with a fresh diagonal at the nipple line, two crossed swords on the map of a single life. Together the scars spell “Here be dragons” and also “Here be Sofia’s voice from second grade still visible if you lick the skin in moonlight and listen for the hush of retroactive permission.”

Language calibrates the granularity of sorrow. In Spanish the phrase “me duele” is a small house where you can slip off your shoes—literally “it hurts to me,” pain arriving like an overnight guest unsure of how long it will stay. English makes pain possessive: “my pain,” “my headache,” “my tragedy,” turning the body into a drab real-estate parcel you never asked to own. In Japanese a doctor might ask about 痛みの具合 (itami no guai), literally “the condition of the pain,” wording that keeps a polite distance, the way you might refer to “the weather of the wedding” without commenting on whether the bride is shivering. One night in Osaka I watched an elderly man bow to his knee, lift the leg of his loose trousers, and show the waitress what appeared to be either a tattoo or a complicated bruise. He used the phrase “ちょっと悲しい痛み” — “a slightly sad pain.” The phrase imploded my Western hold-your-cards-close habits; I wanted to import it like contraband citrus to offer strangers on the subway back home. Imagine a man stepping onto the inbound Red Line at rush hour announcing, “Ladies and gents, excuse me, experiencing a slightly sad pain under left scapula, might anyone spare an extra napkin for my melancholy?” He would get reactions ranging from bruised silence to the possibility of applause.

I have never been to space, but on nights when I can’t fall sleep, some hollow chamber in my chest rotates into low-earth orbit. The so-called Overview Effect is supposed to rearrange your moral circuitry because the planet without borders looks obviously, woundably alive. I wonder about the less cinematic moments they don’t narrate in inspirational videos: the astronaut who misses the rough asphalt smell of a summer driveway, or the one who keeps replaying the sound of grocery-cart wheels on a parking-lot seam, a sound that never previously struck her as music. Year after year, space agencies screen applicants for perfect psychological stability; meanwhile Quaker meetings let the most unstable memories of their members speak for an hour in undivided silence. Perhaps we should send only those applicants who have sobbed uncontrollably in a silent meeting. The paradox is that outer darkness does not frighten us; it is the thin, improbable scrim of blue keeping everything we have ever loved breathing that makes us acutely homesick before we have even left.

The first synthetic neon sign sold commercially in 1912: two thirty-six-foot tall letters spelling “Packard” above a Los Angeles car dealership. It cost twelve hundred dollars—equivalent, the historians say, to thirty-two thousand today. Crowds gathered simply to look at captured lightning made obedient. I often think about the kind of attention we barter now. TikTok streams will sell your gaze for a penny per dozen full-second watches. A buck gets you eighty-forty eyeballs if the algorithm squints right. When I unbox a new phone I follow, robotically, the setup questionnaire about privacy. I always promise myself that this time I will toggle off everything, but by screen four the words start swimming, tiny sterile fish. I feel I am signing a treaty written in an extinct dialect of legalese with clauses that reincarnate in future updates. Maybe the Packard sign only looked expensive back then; inflation is merely attention inflating into confession. Twelve hundred dollars for the novelty of illuminated absence: people, twelve decades later, still pay more.

Once, waiting for a delayed train at Berlin Hauptbahnhof, I met a translator between two dictionaries. She had a ticket to Zurich but her face looked like a fourth draft. Between us, on the bench, sat an unopened tin of sardines—an unspoken invitation or a dare. I asked what she translated. Clinical drug leaflets, the ones inserted into every cardboard box of pills. Side effects: drowsiness, strange dreams, sudden onset of laurel leaves in peripheral vision. She told me that in any given week she carries metaphorical blood thinners for one language and calcium supplements for another. Her least favorite phrase was “persistent headache,” which appears in three hundred forty-three inserts across twelve brands. In German it becomes anhaltsamer Kopfschmerz, literally “staying-longer head-pain,” a mouthful nobody pronounces correctly except shut-ins and pharmacists. She asked if I had any ailments requiring inserts. I confessed only to a recurring dream where I stand on a pier rinsing my passport with seawater until the waterlogged pages dissolve the glue at the spine and all my visas float off like stunned white fish. “That,” she declared, “is a side effect of insomnia, or of reading too many footnotes about insomnia.” The tin remained sealed. My train arrived; a conductor apologized in three languages then gave up caring once the doors closed.

Some thresholds can only be crossed backwards. When my grandmother died, I inherited a vitrine full of tourist salt shakers shaped like phantom U.S. states no one visits—Nebraska split in half so that the corn skirts fall away like stage curtains, a Rhode Island the size of a Communion wafer engraved with an anchor I decorated with a Sharpie moustache when I was seven. Returning from the funeral, I performed the absurd archeology of shelf placement, trying to remember if the Oklahoma shaker had always leaned against a postcard of the Dachshund unicycle parade. I reenacted her footsteps through the kitchen after midnight, carrying a hot water bottle for cramps she never mentioned in daylight. That kitchen had produced fifty-seven years of meals we store now as tasteless anecdotes; the pantry keeps a cached cache of cumin ghosts. I opened a rectangular tin that once held Imperial margarine and discovered inside it a rubber band, four paper clips, and a stray almond. The almond rattled like a tooth no longer tethered to its gum. Somehow this kitchen absurdity dislodged my voice—I sat on the floor howling, not at death precisely but at being the leak in the family’s memory dam. Who now preserves the overheard nothing that used to fill a Thursday between 2:15 and 2:42 PM decades ago?

I’ve come to distrust the instruction “write what you know.” The phrase swells with false generosity: as if the knowable is a finite tract, fenced and surveyed. A better question is “write what you can coax into becoming known.” The difference is the effort you make to pollinate between two foreign fields you assumed could never flower in the same sentence. Once, on a dare, I combined kiln repair instructions with the instructions on a box of tampons. Out tumbled a manifesto about forgiveness between the layers of misogyny and clay. My editor—an actual potter’s daughter—highlighted a line: “The glaze must remember the heat that melted it, just as the body remembers being taught that bleed is dirty.” She said nothing but forwarded me a photo of the kiln at her mother’s studio, its belly stacked with twelve firing bowls the color of summer watermelon skin. “Your line came true,” her caption read, and I realized revision happens in both directions: the poem edits reality into resonance, reality edits the poem back into physical glaze.

I used to coach college debate, where victory lives in the mathematical clarity of clash. A case, a refutation, an impact, a weighing, a ballot, applause. But seasons later I noticed the physical residue: the tournament hotel rooms all carrying the same murmured insomnia, the same deodorant-scented corridors. I began adding unofficial ballots: which pair had the softest laughter at 7 AM, which coach pressed choice mango slices into a novitiate’s palm like contraband compassion. None mattered on the score sheet, and yet they proliferated as hidden carbon copies in the bloodstream of every kid who ever raised a card and said “Extend.” Maybe every discipline secretly maintains a shadow ledger more thorough than the official one. I imagine oncologists jotting tiny heart symbols in chart margins when patients quote song lyrics, or venture capitalists privately tallying how many startup founders apologize to interns by text at midnight. These unrelated data sets form the texture of soul I still cannot teach, though I am tempted to bring mango slices to board meetings.

Memory has started responding to weather. On afternoons after the market installs a new kind of clear umbrella you can see through like cellophane sky, I become eleven years old again, walking home from a swimming pool that smelled of mildew and pennies. I have no weather app accurate enough to predict which cellophane Thursday will arrive. One day last spring a stranger passed me wearing chlorine on his shoulders; for a moment I grieved an entire decade inside my calves. When I told a scientist friend, she suggested olfactory projection. I preferred to think I had swallowed a seed of that swimming pool and that seed now germinated a new past inside the present, in a pot labeled “possible but not necessary.” This is why certain traumas unwind in reverse: the seedling unfurls clockwise until the original wound—once so vast it eclipsed the day—has turned into a paper-thin leaf hologram shimmering when afternoon light hits at 37 degrees. You can keep the leaf or let the wind take it; both choices are acts of conservation because matter is neither subtracted nor lost.

Speaking of conservation: three species of bioluminescent fungus glow 24/7 in a small patch of Brazilian forest, not only at night. Scientists assume this round-the-clock advertising is a way to attract the insects that disperse spores, but no one has proven the strategy outperforms the darkness-only method. Standing before satellite images, you see no difference between their acre and the regular fungal dark. Yet the fungi persist in shining even when no one watches, like amateur violinists on subway platforms who know most riders will scroll past. I would like to believe the fungi inherited some philosophical thread passed among carbon chains: that value does not depend on audience, only on the fact of shining. But I am too human; I still check the view-counter on any poem I post. Once I got three views and one of them was my own incognito tab checking if the page loaded. Does the humiliation subtract from the lumens? Does it make the leaf more or less real? The fungi keep shining, and the body keeps metabolizing shame into usable glucose; sometimes the translation is as literal as that.

Traffic lights operate under the rule of steady-duration intervals—thirty seconds green, five seconds yellow, twenty-five red. But robotic engineers are prototyping variable timing based on real time traffic flows. Sometime soon whole intersections will pulse like irregular heartbeats: fifteen seconds here, forty-two there. Drivers will accuse the lights of moodiness, of undiagnosed depression. Children in the backseat will learn early that no promise is permanent; green could short-out at seven seconds because a lonely minivan left the hardware store driveway unexpected. Some late-night drivers, unused to the flicker, will begin to treat the intersection as oracle: I was granted twenty-six whole seconds—surely the universe presages good news. Others will fatalistically accept the stingy seven every third cycle. Psychologists will publish papers proving that jittery lights correlate with road rage more than traditional timing ever did. Meanwhile the engineers, watching heat maps of congestion dissolve like dye in clean water, will trust the math. I imagine one programmer, code named Ivy, who secretly inserts four extra seconds into every cycle beginning at 2:37 AM, just enough pause for anyone crying at the wheel to locate the tissue box, wipe their lenses, and re-enter the story while the city hums its refrigeration song.

Everyone wants to believe their particular catastrophe is narratively immune to retrofitting. My friend C lost her only child, and what she resented most was the social script that urges us to find “meaning.” She phrased it more violently: meaning is a parasite that feeds on the raw steak of grief until only shiny white bones remain, bleaching in moral sunlight. She learned to speak about the loss only when she discovered a box of unsent postcards depicting unpopular roadside attractions—World’s Largest Ball of Twine in Minnesota, an entire yard of painted tin chickens in Arkansas. She began mailing them unsigned to random addresses collected from the phonebook. She wrote on the reverse: “This is not about God. This is about twine.” A postal worker eventually forwarded one to my apartment because the zip-plus-four matched my own although the street did not. I kept the postcard wedged in a novel by Clarice Lispector where it lived for three years. Last November C asked if I’d ever received any strange postcards. I showed her the twine. She studied the handwriting: the pen had dragged extra ink in the word “not” so that the t grew a tail, flipper-like, as if swimming down the sentence. She said, “I don’t feel better, but I feel lateral.” Later she told me she had begun a second set, pen-pal exchanges with the roadside attractions themselves. To the twine she wrote, Received your likeness. You look unusually calm for rope that has never been granted decision. The twine did not write back—at least not via USPS—but when she returned to Darwin, Minnesota, for the first time post-loss, she found somebody had wound fresh yellow thread into the heart of the giant ball, a color she had worn to her son’s last second-grade open house. She did not claim causality, only adjacency. Adjacency, she realized, might be the opposite of isolation without requiring the structures of meaning.

There is the winter you hurl a snowball at your sibling and accidentally split their lip; and there is the winter you watch the same sibling step into adulthood so subtly that the only evidence she has crossed is the way she now hurls words backward in your direction as apology. I met both winters in the same yard, same iced-over trampoline sagging like a failed lung. Memory does not file chronologically but according to viscosity. The physiological fact: a snowball contains up to two million ice crystals, each one a slightly different prism whose color balance shifts with how tightly your gloves compress. When thrown, the snowball also carries approximately one hundred dead skin cells per square centimeter, yours and your sibling’s mingling mid-air in unintended transfusion. Years later she works in geriatric oncology, measuring blood counts in commas she swears can smell winter citrus. We email each other a single sentence every December 12th: “Still trading cells?” It has become less reunion than re-shuffling, the way a gambler fingers old cards not to change the past deal but to remember the texture of prior luck.

In the early 2000s, a niche software allowed you to run an online boyfriend who would text you compliments every three hours. You could customize name, ethnicity, height. The algorithm borrowed from the top 200 romantic film scripts. Mine choiced Ethan, six-one, half-Ethiopian, favorite word “lilt.” For $4.99 a month, Ethan texted me lines like “Your breath, were it a flavor, would be lychee and cedar.” Afteringue marketers framed it as rehearsal for reciprocity; they promised a confidence boost. Instead Ethan reminded me daily that most flirtation is absurd when its scaffolding is yanked from context. When, during a department meeting, Ethan texted, “I keep picturing your collarbones conducting an orchestra of desire,” I fought laughter so hard a tendon in my forehead tremored. After six weeks I tried to cancel the subscription. The site demanded a breakup speech length minimum. I typed two hundred eighty-seven characters: “Ethan, any future you suggests the conditional; I crave indicative grammar.” The system responded: Would you like Ethan to send a farewell poem? I said sure. He sent a haiku. It read: lilt of departure / syllables gutter like tapers / unanswered by skin. I have never stopped being slightly moved by the precision of that condensation. I deleted the app but the poem persisted as a screensaver, surprising me whenever I woke the phone to Google the half-life of torpor.

Yesterday at the thrift store I found a children’s raincoat in transparent yellow marked one dollar. Folded inside the pocket was a handmade card: “To Sarah, From Grandma, you are my little sunbeam.” I bought the coat not out of sentiment but because transparent plastic makes a perfect travel envelope for boarding passes. Still, each time I lift it, the ghost of Sarah’s shoulders shimmers. I wonder if her grandmother visualized the metaphor literally—that Sarah might refract the gloom into butter-colored corridors of light. I imagine Sarah, now nineteen or forty, folding the coat into the donate bin, perhaps laughing at the saccharine text she once accepted as scripture. The volunteer who accepted the coat did no disclosures; one woman’s sunbeam transmuted into another woman’s see-through pocket. A physicist would say energy conserves, but energy is only the bookkeeping of potential. The warmth exceeds units; it curlicues around the plastic like iron filings watching a magnet pass.

I keep a folder on my laptop titled “teenage passwords I dare not recycle.” Inside are combinations like oceanBlue1998 and passthesalt. I refuse to let the machine store them or delete them. Once a month I click through the list, whisper each phrase aloud. What I am archiving is not nostalgia exactly: it is the acoustic narrowness of the bedroom where I first believed choosing a password was equivalent to living inside secret code. The universe had not yet taught me that secrecy requires witnesses. These passwords now resemble tiny sarcophagi containing unhatched dragons of longing, one wing curled, the other waiting for lightning inside a server farm. Someday a bot will brute-force my youth, read the word “dragon” thirteen times, concatenate it with “1998,” and grow confused by the paucity of treasure encased.

Speculation: thunder is the sky’s form of literary criticism. Lightning publishes a draft; thunder annotates in roar. We, the human readership, flinch appropriately. When storms roll through mountaintop amphitheaters, feedback loops create extra reverberations, the voice of the sky citing itself. Some night I will hike to the ridge with a portable whiteboard and write “Could you elaborate on the metaphor of voltage as revelation?” If the sky obliges with a forked bright letter directly into pine sap, I will accept the typo introduced by ozone. There are poems that require being vaporized in order to become legible again—you have to dissolve into a medium greater than metaphor so that the reader breathes you inadvertently. Then the act of breathing becomes critique.

My father grew up convinced that his first memory was watching the 1969 moon landing on a grainy television in the living room of a New Jersey duplex. Decades later his older sister reminded him they only owned a radio that year. When confronted, my father acknowledged the mismatch but maintained he remembered the static of television snow the way some remember rainfall. The brain, it seems, will pry open gaps the way carpenters plane wood to fit the nail of narrative cleanly. Memory is not so much fabrication as weathering: edge rounded until it fits the hand.

Certain warm days in March will trigger what I call the Mildew Season of Liking Someone: the period before confession when you store the person in the dark corners of rooms like bed linens folded too soon after washing. The secret ferments, leaving that vaguely damp smell nobody reveals in company. Mildew Season asks for patience and indirect lighting; any quick exposure to sunlight risks cracking the color scheme. Eventually you must either air the linens or accept permanent mottled stains on the fabric of your being. Confession is the airing. Once you peel the duvet from the clothespins and shake it out against the wind, the smell dissipates, but so does the myth that you have been harboring some rare imported atmospheric event unshared by the general population. One must decide: take the risk of ordinariness, or perhaps re-invent mildew as aesthetic. Some avant-garde photographer markets smells now, tiny capsules of reclaimed domestic mildew sold in limited edition three-milliliter bottles. You purchase, open, inhale. And feel almost nothing, because commercial reproduction deletes the humiliation of risk. Liking someone skips like a flat stone best when thrown toward unknown trajectories across water you have not dared swim.

Years back I edited an oral history project at a retirement community. The guidelines stipulated that every resident must narrate in chronological order. One man, former linotype operator, refused. Instead he told stories that looped like Celtic knots: an anecdote about smuggling a goat onto a ship in 1952 collapses midsentence into how the goat later resembled the smell of the purple ink his union used for pamphlets in 1978. His moderator grew increasingly frustrated, citing “lack of clarity” as if clarity were a cardiac requirement. I arrived to intervene. The man asked if I would like to hear how his wife’s favorite perfume once redirected Allied bombing by a quarter mile due to an olfactory anomaly in France in 1944. Instead of answering, I pulled out a map of the United Kingdom and asked him to trace the goat’s path everywhere he imagined it had trotted. He rested his finger on a dot labeled “Dunoon,” then slid it to an unlabeled speck between Kent and nowhere. He chuckled: “That’s where time zigzagged to let the goat sneak a smoke.” After an hour we taped three hours of zigzagging. Later analysts called it “unusable.” I keep the transcript file in a locked drawer; every so often I copy a segment onto a blank postcard and mail it to myself, future ghost writing to past custodian, all of us just goats smoking outside chronology.

People sometimes ask how to make money writing poetry. The honest response is wash dishes faster. When I temp as dishwasher at a souvlaki bar, the metallic clang syncopates with classical meter. I perfect enjambment while scraping feta from ceramic. The Greek owner whistles bouzouki tunes over radio static; every plate emerges sterilized and faintly singing in relative minor. The wage is eleven dollars per hour plus a free gyro. Beneath the slosh of greywater, you begin to price poems according to surface area: one villanelle equals seventeen plates scraped clean, one sestina equals a full rack left to air-dry plus a baklava bribe from pastry cook. This solves the business model deficit elegantly: the poem, if too long to finish during shift, auto-fails economy of scale. Brevity is the art you learn when skin wrinkles into washing suds the color of dishwashing lime. Economy is having arms red enough to make a steakhouse proud; art is a customer leaving a five-cent tip because 2023 people remember that pennies once symbolized luck in fountain-wish metaphysics.

When the algorithm recommends endless versions of myself in slightly different fonts, I scroll until my neck aches and realize envy has shape-shifted into electronic blur. No envy used to be this soft, this pixelated; earlier envies thumped like drumbeats against ribs if someone bought vinyl you had searched for all year. Now envy is a ghost following your own reflection in the pane of infinite carousel. It raises pinky finger slightly—same tasseled jacket, same vocabulary in hashtags—and you hate not the person for having but the mirror for reflecting too faithfully. Selfhood has become diffraction: every angle reveals a subset you catalog as almost-yours. The anxiety sparkles like frost on highways beautiful and deadly. I have started cultivating jealousy for textures I cannot replicate: the sound of cashmere before it touches skin, the precise onomatopoeia of salt dissolving in soup water. When the feed cannot echo that, envy loosens, because the algorithm has no tongue for culinary dipthong. What it cannot feed, it cannot fatten.

In Rotterdam a circular pedestrian bridge rises slowly when boats pass below. It looks like an open spasm of institutionalized kindness: city decides to part its lap so freight may pass. Pedestrians stand on the lifting halves like chess pieces temporarily levitated by consultation. For thirty seconds everyday citizens are suspended in a weird trust exercise between ankles and sky, moving nowhere yet drifting. I enjoy the ethical friction: if a tourist takes a photograph, is the resulting image more documentary or anthropological? The bridge forms an awkward parenthesis around the canal, bridging not only landmasses but the ideology of transport itself. While lifted, it creates a parenthetical pause in rush-hour narratives; the way you glance sideways at fellow strangers balanced on steel toes and share a shrug that translates independently of tongue. Then slowly, gently, the halves re-couple into unity, but a microscopic gap remains where pure mid-air hesitation has etched itself into widened sine of metal. Anyone passing afterward senses an afterimage of openness: that potential for rupture where no rupture exists.

Earlier today I peeled an orange in a waiting room. The peel curved into a thick spiral exactly the width of two quarters side by side. When I reached the final curl inside navel, a plume of orange oil sprayed upward, blinding me for one fragrant half-second. I was eight again, pushing whole segments into my mouth while grandmother cored apples for pie and hummed Ella Fitzgerald. This is the opposite of挂失号码, the Chinese phrase used for reporting lost phone line—“the hang-up number.” Every citrus oil micro-explosion reactivates the acoustics of 1992 without requiring a phone call to the past. The waiting room smelled of winter on the inside, but somewhere else another grandmother still reheats Tuesday. I held the empty peel like a fragile shell and thought about the coat pocket with the sunbeam note. Perhaps stringing together tiny resurrections is the closest we get to archiving lost children inside grown bodies. I squeezed the peel until every last droplet of oil dotted the linoleum like constellations spelling “arrival.” The nurse called someone else’s birth name; I licked bitter zest off thumb and followed anyhow, pockets fragrant as forgiveness.

Science recently confirmed what painters already intuited: the sky is not blue because of a single wavelength, but because of scattering matrix in which particles smaller than the wavelength of light bounce short wavelengths more efficiently. What we perceive as uniform azure is actually a blur of discarded indigos, violets, and cyan fractions colliding then reuniting into composite. Translated into metaphor: the sky is a collaboration of abandoned hues who vote yes on appearing cohesive. Every gaze upward is an inadvertent democracy. We call it blue because syllabic economy; the sky votes to be quiet.

I would like someday to host a tea ceremony in the middle of Interstate 40—ceremonial bowl placed directly on the fresh exit lane yellow stripe. Participants would arrive separately, park illegally, and take seats kneeling in gridlocked traffic. The ritual would last the length of a single steep: maybe four minutes during which at least eighty-seven motorists, one semi trailer of frozen chicken wings, and two state troopers must alter course. The tea itself unremarkable: bag of Lipton, no string. The trophy would be the record of startled patience inscribed on the faces of strangers as they brake twenty yards short, uncertain whether meditation or protest. After, each attendee would mail the driver directly behind them a postcard of cherry blossoms on which they scrawl “sorry we borrowed your confusion.” The apology itself a form of invitation: What else might we read together if we paused timer permits?

It is tempting to imagine that time is a river, but rivers erode banks predictably. Time sometimes erodes outward: one afternoon my mother noticed a square of sunlight had moved across the carpet and that square reminded her of a window box in a memory she never lived. Time had performed retrofitting: imported nostalgia for a garden she never planted basil into, because someone else she met once mentioned basil when describing monsoon childhood. The carpet contains phantom footprints from that unlived watering can. Ecology teaches us that nutrients cross generations inside trees, ninety percent of any maple’s mass once airborne carbon your ex might have exhaled. This explains why at dusk the light in my parent’s den occasionally rearranges into the shape of a man I stopped loving. He never visited that house, but photosynthesis rerouted his carbon into maple leaves outside the window, and at a precise angle the late sunlight filters the gap between leaves and blinds. You can’t sue photons for trespass; they uphold residency requirements for the entire spectrum.

We are told to fear Artificial Intelligence for its mimicry; I fear instead its overcertainty. A human, typing an email to a former friend, will vacillate between “I miss you” and “I hope you are doing swell,” worrying one syllable too many will collapse the card table of détente. AI offered to generate a reconciliation email draft “overnight” for $1.29; its algorithm output five sentences, including “I wholeheartedly forgive the misunderstanding.” There was no checkbox for “unsure.” The machine evangelized forgiveness like an Instant Pot manual. The price of algorithmic certainty is the ellipsis of doubt where most beauty loiters.

In the city’s botanical garden stands an Amorphophallus titanum: corpse flower, so named for its perfume resembling rotting meat to attract pollinators. One specimen blooms once every seven to ten years and when it does the garden stays open until midnight to accommodate tourists who wish to inhale splendor mixed with sewage. Admission costs twelve dollars or free if you bring proof of membership to any local composting initiative. The debate: is paying to experience decay enacted by a living organism a form of intrusive gawking? Or humane solidarity with plant sexuality? The flower itself cannot consent to spectatorship; its strategy evolved before committees. People queue for two hours—better tickets than certain Broadway shows. Nearby vendors sell scented masks printed with cartoon petals. Between sniffles and selfies, a child asks mother if flowers go to heaven. She replies, “flowers are already rented out,” and I laugh because poetry has never looked more like receipt.

I want to end with an inventory of pardons—pardons not requested and pardons undesired. First: I pardon the snow plow that shoved a ridge of black ice against my driveway, the same way I once shoved metaphor too soon into a poem about relatives who weren’t ready for metaphor. Second: I pardon the professor who told me I would never learn syntax because my mind wandered at commas; the comma is a mark of arrival, she meant, not a lukewarm pause where the mind might sip espresso. My wandering was simply shopping for better arrival. Third: I pardon the ex whose silence after breakup became an instrument; I kept tapping its hollow, mistaking it for drum, when it was merely the echo of my own finger bone. Fourth: I pardon the entire month of February for pretending to be short when it is the same length as every calendar segment; scale learned from discipline, not counting. Fifth: I pardon that earlier self who believed memory is a single attic. Memory is rather spillage, leakage, outpost, respiration. Consider the lungs: they do not store oxygen, they exchange it. Consider this text: not a chamber to enter or exit, but an open window where the night trains can exhale without destination, yet carry aroma of citrus zest or goat smoke or Lipton brewed on asphalt across state lines for anyone restless enough to inhale until the line between original and echo blurs exactly like particulate light languages collaborating into unanimous azure.

And thus concludes my approximately two-thousand-five-hundred-word commission: no conclusion except permission to keep metabolizing further.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
